review_summary: The segment provides enough textual grounding for core recap questions on tensorized classification, linear-model limits, and backpropagation.

question_bank.question: In an image-classification pipeline, what does the model usually output for each input image?
choices: A vector of scores, one for each predefined category.
A single raw pixel value that represents the whole image.
A hand-written rule tree describing every object part.
A new training image for each possible label.
question_bank.explanation: Classification models turn an input image tensor into category scores. Higher scores indicate classes the model thinks are more compatible with the image.

question_bank.question: Why are plain linear classifiers limited for image recognition?
choices: They collapse each class into a single template and separate space only with linear boundaries.
They cannot produce any numeric score for a class.
They require every image to have exactly one pixel channel.
They always overfit because they have no parameters.
question_bank.explanation: A linear classifier has limited representational power: each class is summarized by one learned template, and the decision regions are linear hyperplanes.

question_bank.question: What makes backpropagation scalable across complex computational graphs?
choices: Each node only needs its forward computation and a local rule for passing gradients backward.
Each layer must know the full architecture of every other layer.
The graph avoids gradients and searches parameters randomly.
The loss is computed separately for every possible network path.
question_bank.explanation: Backpropagation composes local gradient computations through the graph. This lets complex networks be trained without deriving one huge gradient expression by hand.
